title: Wiring and fitting brood frames
subtitle: Making up new frames ready for the hive.
excerpt: A new brood frame needs wax foundation wired in before the bees will draw it out. The wiring keeps the comb straight and stops it collapsing under its own weight once it's full of honey or brood. Here's how to do it cleanly from bare wood to hive-ready.
sourceNotes: BBKA technical data sheets on hive management and frame assembly. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, chapter on frame preparation. NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), hive-maintenance section.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin sheet of wax embossed with the hexagonal cell pattern; workers build comb from it.

content.text: A new brood frame is made up of four pieces of wood, top bar, bottom bar, two side bars, and needs a sheet of wax
foundation

fitted and wired before the bees will draw it out into usable comb. The wire supports the comb once it's heavy with honey or brood; without it, the comb sags or breaks during extraction. Most beekeepers make up frames in batches of ten or twenty at the start of each season.
What you need
Flat-pack brood frames, top bar, bottom bar, two side bars with pre-drilled wire holes
Wax foundation sheets cut to brood-box size (DN4 for a National hive brood box)
28 gauge stainless frame wire, rustproof, pre-cut to length or from a reel
Frame stapler or small nails and a hammer to assemble the woodwork
Embedding tool (spur wheel) or a warm knife to press the wire into the foundation
Assembling the woodwork
Fit the side bars onto the top bar first, pressing the wedge-cut grove at the top of each side bar into the corresponding slot. Nail or staple the joint.
Slot the bottom bar into the bottom of both side bars. Nail each end. The frame should sit square, check the diagonal measurements are equal.
Break off the wedge strip from the top bar. This is the piece that will hold the foundation in place after you've fitted the wire.
Wiring the frame
Thread the frame wire through the pre-drilled holes in the side bars, running horizontally from side to side. Most National frames have four holes per side bar.
Pull the wire taut before securing each end at the last hole on each side. A loose wire won't support the comb. Wrap the wire once around a small staple or frame nail at each end.
Pluck the wires lightly. They should ring, not flop. If a wire goes slack, re-tension it before fitting the foundation.
Fitting the foundation
Lay the wired frame flat. Slide the wax foundation sheet between the wires and into the groove at the top bar, with the raised cell-base pattern facing up.
Nail the wedge strip back in along the top bar to clamp the top edge of the foundation.

Run the embedding spur wheel along each wire in turn, pressing it slightly into the wax. The wire should sit just below the foundation surface, not cutting through it. Work quickly on cold days, the wax cracks if it's brittle.
Check the foundation sits flat between all four wires with no bubbles or folds. A fold draws comb crookedly and the frame won't hang correctly in the hive.
Storage and handling

Finished frames store flat in a cool dry shed. Stacking them upright warps the foundation over time. Don't leave them near a heat source, the wax softens and sags. Use within the season they're made up; old foundation yellows and becomes brittle.

Wax foundation comes in worker-cell size (standard, for brood and honey) and drone-cell size (slightly larger hexagons, used by some beekeepers for targeted varroa management). Most hobbyists use worker-cell throughout. Plastic foundation is also available; it needs a thin coat of wax sprayed on before bees will accept it readily.
What can go wrong

attrs.cue: Foundation droops or sags in the frame
attrs.cause: Wires too loose, or embedding not deep enough to anchor the sheet
attrs.fix: Re-tension the wire and run the embedding tool more firmly. In summer, work in a cooler part of the day.

attrs.cue: Foundation cracks or breaks when embedding
attrs.fix: Warm the sheets briefly near a lamp or in a warm room before working. Cold wax shatters under the spur wheel.

attrs.cue: Bees chew out the foundation rather than drawing it
attrs.cause: Old, contaminated, or incorrectly sized foundation; colony not ready to draw comb
attrs.fix: Use fresh foundation. Only introduce frames when there's a strong nectar flow and the colony is building actively. A small colony won't draw comb quickly.
